prior_lessons_consulted: rsa_can_2026-06-28.json (same question format + same RBP postmortem just settled)

Brazil.group_record: 1W-1D-1L... actually 2W-1D, 7pts, GD+6 (1 goal conceded in 3 matches)
Brazil.status: Group winner, dominant favorite today on every market

Japan.status: Solid but clearly second-tier favorite today; conceded more than Brazil across the group stage

BRA.OFFSIDE_OUTLIER_FLAG: 8 offsides in a single match (vs Haiti) is a major outlier vs 0 and 1 in the other two games — likely opponent-specific (Haiti's high defensive line got repeatedly exploited) rather than representative of Brazil's true rate against a more organized opponent like Japan. Trimmed the offside average down for this match's Q10 modeling rather than using the raw 3.0.
BRA.PRIOR_LESSON_BRA_MAR: do not blindly assume Brazil dominance. bra_mar_2026-06-13.json Q8 ('Will Brazil win the match?') was submitted at 1.00 via personal-conviction override of a researched 0.58-0.60 — Brazil actually DREW 1-1 with Morocco. -51.97 RBP, our worst-ever single result, and the single clearest example of Loss Pattern #2 in STEPS_FOR_HIGH_POINTS.md. Lesson directly applied today: every Brazil-related estimate below is taken straight from the live market, never inflated by conviction that Brazil 'should' dominate just because they're a big favorite on paper.

JPN.DISCIPLINE_NOTE: Very clean disciplinary record (only 1 YC across 3 matches, 0.33/game average) despite a high foul count (up to 20 in one match) — low card-conversion-per-foul rate. Suppresses card expectation somewhat relative to what the foul count alone would suggest.
JPN.PRIOR_LESSON_JPN_SWE: jpn_swe_2026-06-25.json was our best Japan match (+70.49 RBP), won almost entirely on Q1+Q2 (+45.56 combined) by trusting pure ESPN-boxscore Poisson/Skellam reads near-raw with no narrative shading — direct reapplication of WINNING TRAIL. No specific player/market carryover to today's question set, but reinforces trusting today's direct-market numbers near-raw rather than shading them.

context_flags: SECOND KNOCKOUT MATCH analyzed this season — same regulation-scoped question format as RSA-CAN (2026-06-28), which settled yesterday at -9.1 RBP. Two fresh lessons from that postmortem directly applied here: (1) avoid the illiquid-many-contract-sum trap for any-player-X markets (Win Pattern #7) — used for Q2 and Q9 below; (2) do not build multi-step unverified timing-decomposition models when a simpler direct market or ESPN-average model is available — today's card question (Q6) is a plain total-threshold market, not a timing-split, so this risk doesn't recur here.

BRA_OFFSIDE_OUTLIER: Brazil's 8-offside game vs Haiti is very likely opponent-specific (exploiting an unusually high Haitian back line), not a stable rate — trimmed down for Q10's modeling rather than trusted at face value, unlike Colombia's similarly extreme-looking but CONSISTENT (3-then-7) offside pattern from COL-POR which was trusted near-raw specifically because it repeated across two different opponents.

BOTH UEDA AND CUNHA MARKETS ARE GENUINELY LIQUID (bid AND offer both present) — a meaningfully stronger signal than RSA-CAN's Rayners price, which was offer-only and got badly overpriced by us (-26.23 RBP) relative to a much-better-calibrated crowd. Still applied a modest trim to Ueda given Japan's team SOT average (3.67/game) is in a similar range to South Africa's (3.33/game) that burned us yesterday — splitting the difference between trusting the liquid market and respecting the cross-match lesson.

BRA_MAR_DISCIPLINE: never inflate a Brazil-related estimate beyond what the live market shows, regardless of how dominant they look on paper — see PRIOR_LESSON_BRA_MAR above (our single worst-ever result was overconfidence on a Brazil match).

questions.text: Will there be 8 or more total shots on target in regulation?
Will any player record 2 or more shots on target in regulation?
questions.pick_tier: MEDIUM-HIGH CONFIDENCE — MODELED, ILLIQUID-SUM TRAP AVOIDED
questions.reasoning: No usable direct market (same illiquid-many-contract issue as RSA-CAN's brace market). Backed out team SOT lambdas from the team/total SOT markets (BRA≈5.02, JPN≈3.57 derived as total-minus-Brazil) and ran a Poisson-binomial top-player-share decomposition (BRA share 30%, reflecting several attacking outlets; JPN share 35%, more reliant on Ueda as focal point). Model gives 0.63; trimmed slightly for share-assumption uncertainty.

questions.text: Will Japan advance to the Round of 16?
questions.reasoning: Smarkets 'To qualify' market (correctly distinct from the regulation-only FTR, same structure verified for RSA-CAN): Japan = 28.18%.

questions.text: Will there be 9 or more total corner kicks in regulation?
Will a goal be scored before the first hydration break?
Will there be 4 or more total cards shown in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Over3.5 cards = 38.25%. Consistent with Japan's genuinely clean disciplinary record (0.33 YC/game) despite a high foul count — no timing-decomposition needed this time, learning directly from yesterday's Loss Pattern #8 (RSA-CAN's card-timing model was the worst result of that match; today's card question is a plain total threshold, so the market can be trusted directly with no derived steps).

questions.text: Will a substitute score a goal in regulation?
questions.reasoning: No direct market. Used combined total-goal lambda (2.47, from team goal markets) with the same ~13%-per-goal substitute-scorer base rate validated (directionally) in RSA-CAN: P(≥1 sub goal) ≈ 1-(1-0.13)^2.47 ≈ 0.29.

questions.text: Will Matheus Cunha score a goal (excluding own goals) in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Genuinely liquid market (bid 0.274, offer 0.2778) — mid = 0.2759. No illiquidity discount needed.

questions.text: Will any player score more than 1 goal (excluding own goals) in regulation?
questions.pick_tier: MEDIUM CONFIDENCE — MODELED, ILLIQUID-SUM TRAP AVOIDED
questions.reasoning: Same Win Pattern #7 methodology that won +11.94 RBP yesterday on the equivalent RSA-CAN question: team goal lambdas (BRA≈1.60, JPN≈0.86) decomposed via Poisson-binomial top-scorer-share (40%/45%) rather than summing the illiquid 'Player to score 2+ goals' contract list. Gives P(BRA brace)=0.136, P(JPN brace)=0.059, combined 0.186.

questions.text: Will either team be ruled offside before the first hydration break?
questions.reasoning: No structured source exists (same confirmed gap as RSA-CAN). Combined ESPN offside average, but trimmed Brazil's contribution down from the raw 3.0 average to ~2.0 to discount the Haiti-game outlier (8 offsides, likely opponent-specific) — combined λ_full≈3.33, scaled to the ~24-minute pre-break window gives λ_pre24≈0.84, P(≥1)=0.57. Trimmed further for general no-market uncertainty and a cautious knockout-match start.

questions.text: Will the match have 3 or more total goals in regulation?
Will Brazil score in both halves of regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market that exactly matches the question wording: 'Brazil to score in both halves' Yes = 30.02%. Taken straight from the market per the BRA_MAR lesson — no conviction-based inflation of Brazil's attacking output.

questions.text: Will Ayase Ueda have at least 1 shot on target in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Genuinely liquid market (bid 0.463, offer 0.495) — mid = 0.479. Trimmed modestly given Japan's team SOT average (3.67/game) sits in a similar range to South Africa's (3.33/game), whose forward (Rayners) was badly overpriced by us yesterday at a similar level. The liquidity here (two-sided market, unlike Rayners' offer-only price) is real grounds for more trust, so only a partial trim rather than a full RULE15-style suppression.

questions.text: Will regulation end in a tie?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Full-time result Draw = 25.90%. Note Brazil HAS drawn once already this tournament as a big favorite (vs Morocco) — this isn't ignored, it's exactly why we're trusting the market's real draw price rather than assuming a dominant Brazil performance suppresses it further.

questions.text: Will Brazil score 2 or more goals in regulation?

strongest_picks: Q3: Japan advance to R16 = 0.28 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — direct 'To qualify' market, correctly distinct from the regulation-only FTR)
Q9: Any player brace = 0.19 (MEDIUM CONFIDENCE — direct reapplication of yesterday's vindicated Win Pattern #7 methodology, avoiding the illiquid-contract-sum trap)
Q6: 4+ cards = 0.38 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — plain direct market, deliberately NOT building a timing-decomposition model after yesterday's Loss Pattern #8)

post_match_results.note: Solid win, but with a clear directional bias in the misses: every single below-crowd result was on a YES outcome where we were too conservative relative to the crowd on Brazil's attacking volume (goals, SOT, cards, sub-scoring, total goals). All the beat-crowd wins were predominantly NO outcomes where staying below the crowd was correct. Net positive only because the modeled (non-direct-market) picks — any-player-brace, Brazil-scores-both-halves, Japan-advance — were very well-calibrated and outweighed the volume-suppression misses.

Q2.question: 8+ total SOT?
Q2.notes: Part of a directional pattern: we under-shot Brazil's attacking volume across Q1/Q2/Q7/Q12, likely from over-applying the 'elite teams in clinical/low-event wins' lesson that was specifically derived from ARGENTINA (ARG-AUT, JOR-ARG), not Brazil. This looks like a cross-team extrapolation error — a known failure mode (see data-source-reliability memory's 'Cross-Team GD1 Extrapolations' Tier-3 warning) applied here across an entire match rather than one question.

Q10.question: Any player brace?
Q10.notes: Direct reapplication of the Win Pattern #7 team-level Poisson/binomial decomposition (avoiding the illiquid-contract-sum trap) — confirmed correct for a second straight match.

Q13.question: Brazil scores both halves?
Q13.notes: BIGGEST WIN. Direct market trust, taken explicitly per the BRA-MAR lesson against inflating Brazil's dominance narrative — paid off clearly.

Q14.question: Ueda 1+ SOT?
Q14.notes: The deliberate trim below the liquid market mid (0.479->0.44), applied out of caution from the RSA-CAN Rayners lesson, ended up costing a small amount here — Ueda's liquid market was actually closer to correct than our lesson-based trim. Contrast with NED-MAR's Brobbey, where trusting the liquid market directly (no trim) won big. Tentative refinement: a genuinely two-sided liquid market should be trusted MORE, not trimmed, even when a surface-level team-stat parallel exists to a previous loss.
